notes.anchor: Dolabella up till now I had only held in high regard
notes.note: P. Cornelius Dolabella, Cicero's son-in-law (he married Tullia in 50 BC); though the marriage was troubled and later dissolved, Dolabella was a Caesarian officer who used his influence to secure the pardon and return of Trebianus, earning Cicero's deep gratitude here.

notes.anchor: not having failed him in his dangers
notes.note: Cicero had defended Dolabella against a charge of adultery brought by P. Valerius Triarius (54 or 50 BC); Dolabella owed him the debt of an acquittal, and this letter acknowledges that the balance has now been more than repaid.

Gaius Vestorius, a wealthy businessman of Puteoli and long-standing friend of both Cicero and Atticus; he appears repeatedly in Cicero's correspondence as a link to the business community of Campania.

Siro, an Epicurean philosopher based at Naples (Neapolis) and teacher to Virgil among others; Cicero speaks of him with warm respect, suggesting a close personal friendship despite his own Academic allegiance.

notes.anchor: You have acquired more of standing than you have lost of property
notes.note: A characteristic Ciceronian consolation-reversal: the loss of fortune (res familiaris) is presented as the lesser evil, since dignitas — civic standing and reputation — has been restored by the pardon, and dignitas was the very thing Pompeians like Trebianus had staked their lives upon.
